narrative:
Unseasonably warm temperatures continue today. The morning is pleasant after some early drizzle, but widely scattered showers develop this afternoon with a rumble of thunder possible. Clouds linger into tonight. A stray evening shower is possible, followed by showers possibly creeping into the western edge of the Appalachian Mountain region late tonight.

Sunday has lots of clouds with showers possible, mostly on the west side of the mountain region. Temperatures remain above seasonal averages Sunday, but they are cooler than today. By Monday, warmer, dry weather returns.

Daylight Saving Time begins tonight, so remember to set your clocks ahead by 1 hour,

Forecast Discussion

he Bermuda High remains in the Atlantic, but some complexities arise this weekend. A backdoor front stalls to our northeast before returning north. Additionally, a front to our west moves through Sunday.

After a pleasant morning (but a touch of early drizzle in spots), clouds increase this afternoon with widely scattered showers. Thunder is possible. A stray shower could linger into the evening, then showers may creep in from the west later tonight.

Sunday has lots of clouds with a few showers, mostly on the west side of the Appalachians. A rumble of thunder remains possible. Temperatures are noticeably cooler Sunday but still above seasonal averages.

Monday through Wednesday, the Bermuda High again becomes the dominant feature in our weather. Temperatures become warm and warmer through the period. Isolated showers are possible again Tuesday afternoon.

Wednesday brings a reminder that the calendar still says “winter.” A sharp cold front arrives Wednesday night with showers and thundershowers. Sharply colder air arrives late Wednesday night.

Thursday is much colder. Even snow showers and flurries are possible, especially west.

This cold snap is a quick hitter. Temperatures are somewhat milder by Friday afternoon and even nicer for a couple of days after that.

We are still looking at around St. Patrick’s Day or a little later for a more important cold outbreak.
